abstract: Despite the vast potential of porous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), their integration into electronic devices has remained limited, primarily due to the scarcity of electrically conductive variants. In this work, we introduce conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs as a versatile class of materials for the chemiresistive detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). We demonstrate that a series of structurally analogous 2D MOFs can be effectively organized into a cross-reactive sensor array, enabling the clear discrimination of diverse VOC categories. Our experimental results reveal the presence of multiple, highly orthogonal sensing mechanisms, suggesting that these 2D MOFs are mechanistically distinct from traditional chemiresistive materials. These findings highlight the unique advantages of conductive MOFs and establish a robust foundation for their use in next-generation chemical sensing technologies.
